title: Christmas cake
subtitle: Rich matured British fruit cake with marzipan and royal icing
excerpt: A deep, fruit-laden Christmas cake made with raisins, sultanas, currants, glacé cherries, and mixed peel soaked in kirsch, baked slowly, and fed with more kirsch weekly as it matures. Apply marzipan one week before serving, royal icing three days before. Needs at least four weeks to mature.
sourceNotes: The British Christmas cake is documented from at least the seventeenth century as an enriched, spiced, dried-fruit cake kept for weeks or months before serving. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the Christmas cake chapter documents a deep, creamed-butter fruit cake with mixed spice, dried fruit soaked in brandy, and the practice of 'feeding' the finished cake weekly with alcohol as it matures. Hannah Glasse, The Art of Cookery Made Plain and Easy (1747), Project Gutenberg: the chapter on rich cakes documents a fruit cake with six eggs, dried currants, and spirits, matured wrapped in cloth before serving. The marzipan and royal icing finish is documented in Victorian domestic-cooking manuals as standard for the formal Christmas table.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the un-iced cake wrapped in marzipan and foil. Defrost at room temperature overnight. Apply royal icing after defrosting.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cake must be made at least 4 weeks before serving. Feed with 2 tablespoons of kirsch weekly, re-wrapping each time. Apply marzipan 1 week before serving and royal icing 3 days before.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf. Cover with a double layer of baking paper after 1 hour to prevent the top from burning. The internal temperature should reach 92°C after approximately 3 hours

glossaryTerms.definition: The practice of spooning small amounts of alcohol over a matured fruit cake at weekly intervals while it is wrapped and stored. The alcohol slowly penetrates the crumb, preserving it and deepening the flavour. Poke holes in the surface with a skewer before feeding so the liquid reaches the centre. A cake fed weekly for four weeks will have a noticeably richer flavour than an unfed one.

content.text: A Christmas cake is a project, not an afternoon bake. The fruit soaks overnight before the batter is mixed; the cake bakes slowly for three hours; then it matures for weeks, fed with kirsch. The marzipan and icing go on in the final stretch. None of this is technically difficult — it is mostly waiting. The result is a cake that cannot be produced in a shorter time: the depth of flavour requires the maturation period.

items.prepNote: for soaking, plus 2 tbsp per feeding

content.text: Combine all the fruit and nut ingredients in a large bowl. Pour over the {{kirsch}}, stir well, cover, and leave overnight (or up to a week for deeper flavour).

Preheat the oven to 150°C fan. Double-line the tin with baking paper on the base and sides. Beat the {{unsalted-butter}} and {{muscovado-dark}} together until combined — this is a dark, dense batter, not a pale cream. Beat in the {{eggs}} one at a time, then the {{black-treacle}}. Fold in the {{plain-flour}}, {{mixed-spice}}, {{ginger-ground}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}}. Fold in all the soaked fruit. The mixture will be very stiff and dark.

Spoon into the tin and level the surface. Bake for approximately 3 hours. Cover the top with a double layer of baking paper after the first hour. The cake is done when a skewer comes out clean and the internal temperature reads 92°C. Cool completely in the tin. Poke holes all over with a skewer and pour 2 tablespoons of {{kirsch}} over the surface. Wrap tightly in greaseproof paper, then foil.
Unwrap the cake once a week and spoon 2 tablespoons of kirsch over the base. Re-wrap and return to the tin. This is
: it preserves and flavours the crumb over the maturation period. Four weeks is the minimum; eight weeks gives a noticeably richer result.

One week before serving: mix the {{almond-flour}}, {{icing-sugar}} for the marzipan, and {{egg-whites}} for the marzipan to a smooth paste. Brush the cake with a little kirsch and press the marzipan over the top and sides. Leave uncovered for 24 hours to dry. Three days before serving: beat the {{egg-whites}} for the icing until frothy, then beat in the {{icing-sugar}} for the icing in stages until thick and glossy. Spread over the marzipan and leave to set hard.
